lineagefreq 0.2.0

Tracking Variant Replacement in Three Lines of R

Source: blog-post.md

In late 2023, the JN.1 sublineage of SARS-CoV-2 rose from undetectable levels to over 80% of sequenced isolates in the United States within roughly ten weeks. Public health agencies needed to know how fast it was growing, when it would dominate, and whether their sequencing volumes were sufficient to track it. These questions recur with every new variant — and they are, at their core, questions about lineage frequency dynamics that can be answered with well-established statistical models.

lineagefreq, now on CRAN, is an R package for estimating growth advantages, fitting frequency trajectories, and generating short-term forecasts from genomic surveillance count data. It was designed around a specific principle: the path from raw sequencing counts to actionable estimates should be short, reproducible, and honest about uncertainty.

The Statistical Foundation

When two pathogen lineages co-circulate, their relative frequencies over time follow a logistic trajectory under the assumption of constant fitness differences. The multinomial logistic regression (MLR) model formalises this: observed sequence counts at each time point are multinomially distributed, with log-linear frequency trajectories parameterised by lineage-specific growth rates. The growth rate difference between a focal lineage and a reference lineage can be converted to a relative effective reproduction number Rt using the generation time (Piantham, Linton, and Nishiura 2022).

This framework is not new. What lineagefreq contributes is a unified, well-tested implementation with built-in evaluation infrastructure that lets analysts move from data to validated forecasts without assembling bespoke pipelines.

From Counts to Growth Advantages

The core workflow begins with lfq_data(), which validates and standardises raw count data, computes per-timepoint frequencies, and flags unreliable periods with low sequencing volume:

library(lineagefreq)
data(cdc_sarscov2_jn1)

x <- lfq_data(cdc_sarscov2_jn1, lineage = lineage, date = date, count = count)

Model fitting uses a single interface regardless of the underlying engine:

fit <- fit_model(x, engine = "mlr")

Growth advantages are extracted in whichever parameterisation the analyst prefers — raw growth rates, relative Rt, selection coefficients, or doubling times:

growth_advantage(fit, type = "relative_Rt", generation_time = 5)

On CDC surveillance data covering the BA.1 to BA.2 transition, the model recovers a relative Rt of approximately 1.34, consistent with published household transmission estimates from Denmark (Lyngse et al. 2022).

Five Engines, One Interface

Not every surveillance question calls for the same model. lineagefreq provides five estimation engines behind a common fit_model() interface:

Engine Type Use case
mlr Frequentist Quick estimates, single location
hier_mlr Hierarchical Multiple locations with sparse data
piantham Frequentist Biologically interpretable Rt
fga Bayesian (Stan) Full posterior uncertainty
garw Bayesian (Stan) Time-varying fitness landscapes

The hierarchical MLR engine applies DerSimonian–Laird random-effects meta-analysis across locations, shrinking noisy site-specific estimates toward the pooled mean. This is particularly valuable for early-phase surveillance, when individual countries or states may have few sequences.

Honest Forecasting

Frequency forecasts are generated by propagating uncertainty from two sources: parameter estimation error (via the Fisher information matrix) and multinomial sampling variability at the effective weekly sequencing volume:

fc <- forecast(fit, horizon = 28, n_sim = 1000)
autoplot(fc)

The result is a set of prediction intervals, not point forecasts. This matters operationally — a 4-week forecast with wide intervals communicates something fundamentally different from one with narrow intervals, and downstream decisions (surge planning, vaccine updates) should reflect that distinction.

Built-in Backtesting

Perhaps the most important feature for methodological credibility is backtest(), which implements rolling-origin out-of-sample evaluation following the framework of Abousamra, Figgins, and Bedford (2024):

bt <- backtest(x, engines = c("mlr", "piantham"), horizons = c(7, 14, 21, 28))
sc <- score_forecasts(bt, metrics = c("mae", "rmse", "coverage", "wis"))
compare_models(sc)

This procedure repeatedly fits the model on historical data, generates forecasts, and compares predictions to held-out observations. It prevents the common mistake of reporting in-sample fit as forecast accuracy. On the BA.2 transition data, 2-week MAE runs approximately 4 percentage points, rising to 8 percentage points at 4 weeks — credible performance for a model with no epidemiological covariates beyond sequence counts.

Surveillance Utilities

Two additional functions address practical surveillance operations. sequencing_power() computes the sample size required to detect a variant at a specified prevalence with a given confidence level — answering the recurrent question of whether a country’s sequencing volume is adequate. summarize_emerging() scans all lineages for statistically significant growth trends, flagging those exceeding a frequency threshold.

Included Data

The package ships with four datasets: two simulated (SARS-CoV-2 US 2022 dynamics and influenza H3N2 clades) and two real (CDC biweekly data covering the JN.1 emergence and the BA.1-to-BA.2 transition). The real datasets enable immediate, reproducible validation against published epidemiological estimates.

Relationship to Existing Tools

Several groups have implemented multinomial logistic models for variant frequency estimation. The Nextstrain/Hubbard group’s evofr package provides Bayesian renewal models in Stan. CDC’s internal pipelines use related approaches. lineagefreq is not a replacement for these specialised tools — it is a lightweight, CRAN-distributed alternative aimed at surveillance teams, academic labs, and public health analysts who need a self-contained solution with minimal setup overhead.

The package follows tidyverse conventions throughout. Broom methods (tidy(), glance(), augment()) ensure that model outputs integrate directly into existing analytical pipelines. The engine registration system allows third-party models to be plugged into the same fit_model() interface, following the design philosophy of parsnip.

Installation and Resources

install.packages("lineagefreq")

Four vignettes cover the core workflow, real-data case studies, model comparison, and end-to-end surveillance pipelines. Source code is at GitHub.

The underlying statistical models are mature and well-characterised. The contribution here is engineering: a clean, tested, documented implementation that lets an epidemiologist go from raw sequence counts to growth estimates, forecasts, and power calculations in a single R session.

Cuiwei Gao is a health data analyst and R developer. lineagefreq is available on CRAN.
